For a while crypto was probably the highest bidder for celebrity and influe=
nce. A ton of money flowed into crypto over the last few years. Margins wer=
e high; it=E2=80=99s not like crypto firms had to build a lot of factories =
and hire a lot of workers to make new tokens. Crypto went up based on socia=
l adoption, on widespread respectability, on the prospect=C2=A0of increasin=
g legality. If you were a crypto firm in 2022, funneling gobs of money to p=
oliticians and former regulators and celebrities was a really good investme=
nt in making your firm popular and respectable, and it didn=E2=80=99t reall=
y compete that much with=C2=A0other=C2=A0investments. If you are Apple Inc.=
, it is good to have regulators on your side and celebrities endorsing your=
 products, but also you make phones and if you spend a lot of money making =
the phones better then customers will want more of them. For large parts of=
 the crypto ecosystem that, uh, was not really an option. Influencer market=
ing and political lobbying were valuable and viable; making useful products=
 that appealed to consumers in an immediate and intuitive way was not.
And so here=C2=A0is  a New York Times story about some guys who were in the=
 celebrity-and-influence business and were at the top of their game:

A former aide to President Clinton, [Michael] Kives started working for Cre=
ative Artists Agency in 2003, representing actors like Bruce Willis and Arn=
old Schwarzenegger. At Mr. Kives=E2=80=99s 2019 wedding in Palm Springs, Ca=
lif., [Katy] Perry sang =E2=80=9CHava Nagila,=E2=80=9D a Jewish folk song, =
and two writers on =E2=80=9CThe Office=E2=80=9D delivered a roast.
Mr. Kives started K5 in 2018. He was later joined by [Bryan] Baum, an entre=
preneur who had co-founded a software company that was acquired by Lyft. K5=
 has invested in start-ups including SpaceX and Boring Company, which are r=
un by Elon Musk, amassing a portfolio of 148 companies.
As they built K5, Mr. Kives and Mr. Baum touted their connections, includin=
g with the billionaire financier Warren E. Buffett.
  [1]


Their firm, K5 Global, is an investment firm, and I suppose that running an=
 investment firm is one way to monetize having a lot of connections to the =
rich and famous: Your connections can help with raising money and also with=
 getting into hot deals; =E2=80=9CI am good friends with Elon Musk=E2=80=9D=
 is one way to get differentiated (
not always good!) investment performance in recent years.=C2=A0
But a better way to monetize those connections was apparently to  sell them=
 to Sam Bankman-Fried:

Mr. Kives, a Hollywood agent turned investor, played an unusual role in Mr.=
 Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s business empire: super connector. He and his busin=
ess partner, Bryan Baum, helped the young founder cultivate relationships w=
ith Mr. Bloom, Ms. Perry and former President Bill Clinton, and offered int=
roductions to a who=E2=80=99s who of celebrities and business leaders, from=
 Leonardo DiCaprio to the governor of Saudi Arabia=E2=80=99s Public Investm=
ent Fund.
The relationship was mutually beneficial. Mr. Bankman-Fried invested $700 m=
illion in Mr. Kives=E2=80=99s venture-capital firm, court records show, an =
extraordinary level of support for a fund with a short track record of star=
t-up investments. Mr. Kives, the founder and face of the firm, and Mr. Baum=
 each received $125 million as part of the deal.
Mr. Kives, 42, and Mr. Baum, 34, were part of an under-the-radar network of=
 middlemen who introduced celebrities and other power brokers to the once h=
igh-flying crypto industry. Before cryptocurrencies crashed last year, a dr=
umbeat of endorsements from Hollywood stars, professional athletes, busines=
s titans and politicians created an aura of credibility around the volatile=
 and largely unregulated market. ...
On Thursday, FTX=E2=80=99s bankruptcy lawyers sued Mr. Kives=E2=80=99s firm=
, K5 Global, to recover the money that Mr. Bankman-Fried had invested. The =
$700 million was among the largest sums the FTX founder contributed to any =
group; the lawsuit said he had made the investment =E2=80=9Cto burnish his =
own political and social influence.=E2=80=9D

One hundred twenty-five million dollars! Each!  The lawsuit makes it clear =
that FTX paid them that money for some of their shares in their businesses,=
 not as=C2=A0personal commissions, but it comes to about the same place. =
=E2=80=9CNo due diligence or financial analysis was conducted to support th=
is inflated valuation=E2=80=9D for the K5 businesses, says the complaint,=
=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cand Kives and Baum knew that this valuation was not well fou=
nded and was materially inflated.=E2=80=9D
The complaint is generally a fun read:

Kives and Bankman-Fried first connected via email on November 16, 2021, whe=
n Kives helped introduce Bankman-Fried to world-renowned Australian musicia=
n Sia. Shortly thereafter, Kives began inviting Bankman-Fried to dinner par=
ties with Wall Street titans, Academy Award winning actors, and NBA stars. =
On February 11, 2022, the Friday before the Super Bowl, Bankman-Fried atten=
ded a dinner party at Kives=E2=80=99s house alongside former high-ranking p=
oliticians, a centibillionaire CEO, and various other celebrities. The next=
 day, February 12, 2022, Kives connected Bankman-Fried via email to a tech =
company CEO and his wife, a former U.S. Ambassador to the Bahamas.
On February 13, 2022, Bankman-Fried attended the Super Bowl as Kives=E2=80=
=99s guest, along with A-list celebrities. Bankman-Fried was captivated by =
Kives=E2=80=99s star-studded circle of friends, and two days later raved in=
 an internal document that K5 Global was =E2=80=9Csomething of a one-stop s=
hop for relationships that we should utilize.=E2=80=9D Bankman-Fried noted =
that Kives and Baum wanted several things in exchange, including =E2=80=9C[=
m]aybe us to invest in them,=E2=80=9D and, as described below, such investm=
ents quickly followed.

Alameda Research Ltd., Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s trading firm, invested about=
 $300 million in K5 entities and about $400 million in some K5 funds, which=
 made venture investments with the money:
As of September 18, 2022, K5X Fund I LP had invested $280.5 million. The va=
st majority=E2=80=94$252 million=E2=80=94was invested in just eight compani=
es, two of which were SpaceX ($189.7 million invested) and The Boring Compa=
ny ($23 million invested), both founded by Elon Musk, with whom Kives purpo=
rted to be close friends. These two investments represented approximately 7=
6% of K5X Fund I LP=E2=80=99s portfolio.
And:

Bankman-Fried noted in an internal document that the FTX executive had ques=
tioned whether Baum was acting in FTX=E2=80=99s best interest and had sugge=
sted that this =E2=80=9Creek[ed]=E2=80=9D of Baum =E2=80=9Ctrying to nickel=
 and dime FTX, or scam it a little bit . . . .=E2=80=9D
Despite these concerns, Bankman-Fried defended Baum, describing what he bel=
ieved was the root of the issue: =E2=80=9CIs Bryan an FTX employee, or a ra=
ndom 3rd party? The answer, really, is neither. The answer is that it=E2=80=
=99s sorta complicated and liminal and unclear. Bryan lives in the uncanny =
valley=E2=80=9D (emphasis in original).

Great.=C2=A0In bankruptcy, a company can in some circumstances=C2=A0get bac=
k money that it paid to other companies, if it =E2=80=9Creceived less than =
a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for such transfer or obligation=
=E2=80=9D and was insolvent at the time. And so the FTX bankruptcy estate i=
s suing Kives, Baum and K5 for the money back, because it =E2=80=9Cdid not =
receive reasonably equivalent value in exchange for=E2=80=9D all the money =
it sent them. Which seems right! One hundred twenty-five million dollars! E=
ach! For some party invites!
On the other hand:
=09Ex ante, it is possible that trying to become friends with like Bill Cli=
nton and Elon Musk and the head of the PIF and Sia was the best possible us=
e of FTX=E2=80=99s $700 million. In the last few years, big crypto firms we=
re in a race to achieve respectability and stability before they could be b=
rought down by legal problems and/or by stealing all the money; FTX lost th=
e race, but I=E2=80=99m not sure it was unreasonable for it to pour all of =
its resources into running it. What else was it gonna do?=09Most of what FT=
X/Alameda did, as they slid into bankruptcy, took the form of spending=C2=
=A0real customer money on piles of magic beans. At the end, FTX was left wi=
th a   balance sheet consisting mostly of Serum and MAPS and FTT and other =
quasi-worthless tokens that Bankman-Fried made up. I suppose the stakes in =
K5 are similarly magic-bean-ish, but=C2=A0at least=C2=A0some=C2=A0of the K5=
 stuff was real! FTX put money into K5's funds and=C2=A0apparently got, lik=
e, $189.7 million worth of SpaceX,   which is up since FTX invested, which =
is more than you can say for FTT.=09The complaint says that FTX paid for mu=
ch of Kives=E2=80=99s=C2=A0and Baum's stakes in K5 with =E2=80=9C$250 milli=
on in shares of FTX Trading Ltd. and West Realm Shires, Inc.,=E2=80=9D whic=
h I=E2=80=99m pretty sure are worth zero now. So arguably it did get equiva=
lent value for=C2=A0that. Magic beans for magic beans.

   =20
      Bittrex v. SEC
   =20
 =20

I   like to   say that all cryptocurrency exchanges are doing crimes, and i=
f you=E2=80=99re lucky your exchange is doing only process crimes. But what=
 if it is? Consider this simple hypothetical fact pattern:
=09A crypto exchange has $1 billion of customer money.=09It has stolen none=
 of this money! Good job.=09The exchange also has $50 million of its own mo=
ney, which it very carefully keeps separate from the customer money, with n=
o stealing allowed.=09The US Securities and Exchange Commission fines it $1=
00 million for operating an illegal securities exchange.=09Now the exchange=
 owes customers $1 billion and the SEC $100 million, for a total of $1.1 bi=
llion.=09It has $1 billion of customer money and $50 million of its own mon=
ey, for a total of $1.05 billion, which is less.=09Its liabilities exceed i=
ts assets, so it files for bankruptcy.=09Do the customers get paid in full,=
 or the SEC, or neither?

This problem comes up because =E2=80=9Ccustomer money" is a fuzzier concept=
 in crypto than it is in traditional banking or securities brokerage. When =
you send dollars to a crypto exchange and use them to buy Bitcoins, and you=
 look at your balance on the exchange's website and it says =E2=80=9C1.57 B=
itcoins=E2=80=9D or whatever, you tend to think that those Bitcoins belong =
to you. Frankly the exchange would probably=C2=A0prefer=C2=A0that those Bit=
coins belong to you, for marketing and legal reasons.
But the situation is not particularly clear, and it does seem more likely t=
hat the exchange=C2=A0owns=C2=A0the Bitcoins, and just=C2=A0owes=C2=A0you a=
n unsecured debt: If the exchange doesn=E2=80=99t have enough money to pay =
back all its creditors, you may not get back all your Bitcoins. =E2=80=9CBe=
cause custodially held crypto assets may be considered to be the property o=
f a bankruptcy estate, in the event of a bankruptcy, the crypto assets we h=
old in custody on behalf of our customers could be subject to bankruptcy pr=
oceedings and such customers could be treated as our general unsecured cred=
itors,=E2=80=9D  says Coinbase Global Inc., controversially but correctly. =
And in fact lots of crypto businesses have gone bankrupt in the last year o=
r so, and it=20
does seem like the=20
legal result is mostly that the coins belong to the crypto businesses and t=
he customers are unsecured creditors. Which might mean that they would have=
 to share with the SEC.
Of course the other question here is, would the SEC actually do this? Would=
 the SEC fine a crypto exchange=C2=A0more than all of its own money for ill=
egally trading securities, and go after customers for the rest of the money=
? On the one hand, it seems a little crazy: When it goes after crypto excha=
nges for operating illegal securities exchanges, the SEC=E2=80=99s goal is =
presumably to=C2=A0protect customers (by imposing=C2=A0on crypto exchanges =
the disclosure, conflicts, supervision, etc. rules applicable to securities=
 exchanges), and it would be strange to take customer money as a penalty fo=
r customer-protection failings. On the other hand, I mean, surely the SEC=
=E2=80=99s=C2=A0broader=C2=A0goal is to=C2=A0clamp down on crypto, and send=
ing a message to crypto customers like =E2=80=9Cyour money is not safe at a=
ny crypto exchange, because the SEC might take it=E2=80=9D has a certain de=
terrent effect.
Anyway this is all a=C2=A0bit=C2=A0fanciful, but also a bit not. In April  =
 the SEC sued Bittrex Inc., a crypto exchange, for operating an unregistere=
d securities exchange. Bittrex responded by, among other things, shutting d=
own its US business and=20
putting it into bankruptcy. But all of the customer=C2=A0money is there and=
 accounted for, and Bittrex asked the bankruptcy court to let it process cu=
stomer withdrawals normally.=C2=A0The Petition newsletter reports:
The debtors filed a motion seeking entry of an order authorizing them to ho=
nor withdrawals of crypto by customers. Interestingly, the debtors stated: =
=E2=80=9Cthere may be a dispute between the Debtors and their customers as =
to who owns the cryptocurrencies deposited by customers and stored in the D=
ebtors=E2=80=99 wallets. The Debtors believe that they own the cryptocurren=
cy assets. But, the proposed treatment, where customers will be permitted t=
o withdraw cryptocurrencies equivalent to 100% of the cryptocurrencies they=
 deposited as a distribution (subject to complying with information request=
s to ensure that all governmental rules and regulations are satisfied) avoi=
ds the expense and delay of litigation concerning ownership of those assets=
, while still providing customers, the vast majority of which are individua=
ls, the ability to receive a 100% distribution by withdrawing the cryptocur=
rencies.=E2=80=9D
That is: The customers technically have an unsecured claim on their crypto =
assets, which are technically owned by Bittrex. But there=E2=80=99s plenty =
of money and not much debt so it=E2=80=99s fine, Bittrex will just give the=
m their=C2=A0crypto back anyway.
Except that the SEC is still suing Bittrex, and presumably expects to extra=
ct a large fine. And so the question arises: Can Bittrex give its customers=
 back all their money, or does it have to hang on to some of that money to=
=C2=A0potentially use to pay SEC fines? While the bankruptcy judge did sign=
 the order allowing Bittrex to give its customers their money back, the ord=
er explicitly said that it would not=C2=A0
prohibit the Debtors, any liquidating trustee, plan administrator or any cr=
editor, including the United States, from recovering from any customer the =
value of any cryptocurrency assets or fiat currency such customer receives =
that exceeds the distributions provided by the plan, if such general unsecu=
red creditor or subordinated creditor is not paid in full, and, in such eve=
nt, the entry of this Order shall not constitute collateral estoppel or res=
 judicata of any customers' entitlement to cryptocurrency assets or priorit=
y under any federal or state law.
That is: If the SEC is not paid in full on whatever fines it ultimately ext=
racts, it can go after Bittrex=E2=80=99s customers for the amounts they wit=
hdrew. As Petition says =E2=80=9CSoooooo=E2=80=A6customers can withdraw the=
ir crypto but that crypto is subject to a potential clawback in favor of th=
e regulators? FUN!!=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Again this is all a bit fanciful, and I c=
annot really imagine the SEC suing Bittrex=E2=80=99s customers for withdraw=
ing their money. But it=E2=80=99s keeping the option open.

   =20
      Due diligence
   =20
 =20

I think sometimes about the similarities between these three businesses:
=09Journalism=09Spying=09Fundamental investment analysis

The basic job of many journalists and spies, and of a certain sort of analy=
st, is to develop personal relationships and use them to find out useful in=
formation that no one else knows, and then give that information to your cu=
stomers. If you are a journalist your customers are your readers =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0sometimes =E2=80=9Cthe public,=E2=80=9D but sometimes a rather narrow=
er class of paying subscribers. If you are a spy your customer is your gove=
rnment. If you are an investment analyst, your customer might be your hedge=
 fund (your boss and your clients), or you might be producing research for =
outside customers (basically,=C2=A0several=C2=A0hedge funds).
The boundaries can be porous. There are some=C2=A0very=C2=A0niche financial=
 publications, for instance. Consider this fact pattern:
=09You work for a publication.=09You cultivate deep relationships with the =
employees of a publicly traded company.=09They tell you that the company is=
 cooking its books, and they smuggle out corporate documents proving it.=09=
You write up your findings in a big detailed convincing report, put the hea=
dline =E2=80=9CCompany X Is a Fraud=E2=80=9D on it,=C2=A0and publish it for=
 your readers.

If you work at Bloomberg News or the New York Times, and your readers are m=
illions of subscribers and website visitors, this is pretty clearly =E2=80=
=9Cjournalism,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0and good. If you work at a firm called Special=
ized Stock Research and your readers are two hedge funds who each pay $1 mi=
llion a year for your reports, this is pretty clearly =E2=80=9Cinvestment r=
esearch,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0and regulators might have serious questions about wh=
ether it is=C2=A0insider trading.=C2=A0Insiders at a public company leaked =
information to you because of your long-standing relationship, and then you=
 passed that information on to people who traded on it: That sounds a lot l=
ike a crime.
  [2]
=C2=A0I wonder sometimes what the boundary is: Is there some minimum number=
 of subscribers, or some maximum subscription price, that makes a report =
=E2=80=9Cjournalism=E2=80=9D rather than =E2=80=9Cinsider trading=E2=80=9D?
  [3]

Similarly if your job is to work in China and find out stuff about Chinese =
companies and policymakers and report back what you learned to an American =
audience, you might be a spy (if the audience is the American government) o=
r a journalist (American newspaper readers) or a due diligence consultant (=
American investors). But in each case your activities will be kind of simil=
ar, and subject to misinterpretation:

Expert network consulting company Gerson Lehrman Group has become the lates=
t due diligence firm to cut jobs in China as Beijing intensifies scrutiny o=
f the sector on national security grounds.
US-based GLG, which maintains a network of specialists that global investor=
s can tap for due diligence on transactions, began trimming China staff las=
t month, said several people familiar with the matter.
The reductions come as Beijing cracks down on foreign consultancies this ye=
ar, alarming international investors at a time of growing tensions between =
the US and China. The campaign has made operating in China more difficult f=
or foreign companies, which depend on the consultants to help navigate the =
world=E2=80=99s second-largest economy. =E2=80=A6
Investors and foreign multinationals said the crackdown made performing due=
 diligence for investments and procurement contracts with Chinese partners =
and suppliers difficult.

Of course journalism is also sometimes misinterpreted as spying.=C2=A0

 =20
   =20
      Art shares
   =20
 =20

If a painting is worth $55 million, how much is a tradeable=C2=A0one-millio=
nth fractional share of the painting worth? Three possible answers:
=09$55, because $55 million divided by 1 million is $55.=09More than $55, b=
ecause there are more people in the world with $55 than with $55 million, a=
nd they will all want shares, and a deep liquid publicly traded pool of buy=
ers will make the shares worth more than the painting itself.=09Less than $=
55, because if you buy the painting for $55 million you can hang it over yo=
ur couch, while if you buy a share for $55 you can=E2=80=99t hang anything =
anywhere.

I don=E2=80=99t know which is right. Intuitively, it seems to me that ownin=
g an entire painting offers certain aesthetic and prestige benefits, while =
owning any fraction of a painting does not offer any fraction of those bene=
fits: Hanging nothing over your couch is not one one-millionth as valuable,=
 interior-decor-wise, as hanging a fancy painting over your couch. But I ha=
ve been wrong before, and the rise of crypto and=20
non-fungible tokens does apparently teach the lesson that people will pay f=
or, and derive some sort of prestige and aesthetic benefit from, an entry i=
n a ledger saying that they own a tiny fraction of some object that they ca=
nnot touch or look at.
Also there is a sort of soft no-arbitrage condition, which is that if the s=
hares all trade at, say, $30, then somebody could buy them all for $30 mill=
ion, own the entire painting, and hang it over her couch, at a significant =
discount to its real value of $55 million.
  [4]

Anyway  here=E2=80=99s this:

A company named Artex is launching a roughly $55 million initial public off=
ering of Bacon=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9CThree Studies for a Portrait of George Dy=
er,=E2=80=9D painted shortly after the couple met in 1963. Shares in the po=
rtrait will be sold for around $100 a piece and will list on a specially cr=
eated art stock exchange based in Liechtenstein, giving regular investors t=
he ability to buy and sell shares in a famous artwork on a stock exchange f=
or the first time.=C2=A0
Trading is expected to begin in July. The portrait will then go on public d=
isplay in a museum, rather than be squirreled away in a freeport =E2=80=94 =
high-security, low-tax warehouses where wealthy collectors often store valu=
able works of art.=C2=A0

Part of the pitch here is about reducing transaction costs:
Artex has big plans to float $1 billion worth of paintings over the next fe=
w months, each individual work with its own IPO. The company thinks supply =
will come from private collectors that are looking for a cheaper alternativ=
e to selling through auction houses like Sotheby=E2=80=99s and Christie=E2=
=80=99s, which can charge up to 20% in commission. Artex takes a lower cut =
of 3% of the IPO proceeds plus a small fee every time shares in the Bacon p=
ortrait change hands.
I assume that if you are Sotheby=E2=80=99s or Christie=E2=80=99s, your pitc=
h to collectors is =E2=80=9Cwe will sell your painting to rich art collecto=
rs with whom we have cultivated relationships and who want to hang it in th=
eir houses, which will result in a price that is at least 25% higher than y=
ou=E2=80=99d get by selling=C2=A0fractional shares in your painting to onli=
ne randos who=C2=A0can=E2=80=99t=C2=A0hang it in their houses.=E2=80=9D I d=
on=E2=80=99t know if that is correct, though, and it=E2=80=99s hard to meas=
ure without much data.
Also:
The Bacon portrait also should be a bit easier to cash in and out of as it =
will be publicly traded, and Artex will subsidize up to 3% of market value =
daily to provide liquidity.
In general, =E2=80=9Cwe will open up an exchange for X targeting retail inv=
estors, and we will also be a market maker on that exchange, buying X when =
retail investors want to sell it and selling it when they want to buy it=E2=
=80=9D seems like a risky but usually lucrative business. (It=E2=80=99s a r=
ough description of the modern=20
US retail stock brokerage business; it has also made a lot of fortunes in c=
rypto.) If you are Sotheby=E2=80=99s, the big problem with $55 million pain=
tings is that they trade infrequently (though at huge spreads). If you are =
Artex and you can succeed in making this Bacon painting trade dozens of tim=
es a day (though in small fractional size and at tighter spreads), and you =
get to be in the middle of each trade, that could be a good deal.
Also:
The first publicly listed artwork also raises the tantalizing possibility o=
f a takeover of the Bacon portrait. If a billionaire collector or museum wa=
nts to swoop in with a bid, they must offer a 20% premium to the average cl=
osing price of the shares over the previous 20 trading days, according to r=
ules set by Artex. A rival bidder can then step in and offer even more, tho=
ugh unlike with a typical corporate takeover, shareholders don=E2=80=99t ge=
t to vote on whether to accept the offer.=C2=A0
This is a weird mechanism? Does it imply that the painting=C2=A0ought=C2=A0=
to trade at a discount to its =E2=80=9Creal=E2=80=9D value in private hands=
 (because any private buyer would have to pay a 20% premium)? If you are a =
rich collector, would you rather buy a painting from Artex at a 20% premium=
 to its trading price, or at a Christie=E2=80=99s auction with a 20% commis=
sion?
  [5]

  [1] =E2=80=9CIn an interview, Mr. Buffett said Mr. Kives was a =E2=80=98n=
ame-dropper=E2=80=99 who =E2=80=98might pitch that he has a connection to m=

s.


  [2] Actual US insider trading law *sort of* incorporates a =E2=80=9Cperso=
nal benefit test=E2=80=9D: It=E2=80=99s illegal insider trading if the insi=
der got some benefit from leaking to you, and not if they just did it out o=
f public-spiritedness or whatever. (In fact the original Supreme Court case=
 stating this rule looked a lot like my hypothetical.) This is a bit vague,=
 though, and  the test is pretty much always satisfied these days: Just the=
 personal benefit of feeling good about your relationship can be enough to =
satisfy the test.=20


  [3] To me the interesting example is  the Blaszczak case, where a =E2=80=
=9Cpolitical intelligence consultant=E2=80=9D wrote reports for a tiny grou=
p of high-paying hedge-fund clients about government decisions. He used gov=
ernment officials as sources for those reports, and was prosecuted for insi=
der trading. If he wrote those reports for a larger group of clients who pa=
id less, I think he would have just been a =E2=80=9Cjournalist=E2=80=9D and=
 so not guilty of insider trading. (He was convicted, but the conviction wa=
s reversed on appeal for reasons that aren=E2=80=99t directly related to my=
 questions about the case.)


  [4] This does not work in reverse: If the shares trade at $100, there is =
no corresponding mechanism to capture the difference between the $100 milli=
on market cap and the $55 million real value. This is   true for corporate =
stocks too, by the way: If a company=E2=80=99s stock trades for much less t=
han the present value of its cash flows, some bidder can buy all the stock,=
 take over the company and capture the cash flows; if it trades for much mo=
re than the present value of its cash flows, short sellers can=E2=80=99t do=
 anything to *force* it back to a realistic valuation. By the way, though, =
in corporate stocks there is at least a concept of a realistic valuation: =
=E2=80=9CA company is worth the present value of its cash flows,=E2=80=9D e=
tc. With art a painting is worth what someone will pay for it. In the text =
I say that the painting is worth $55 million, as though that were a real fa=
ct about the world, but that=E2=80=99s not really right.


  [5] Also can you manipulate this automatic-fixed-premium, no-vote mechani=
sm? If liquidity isn=E2=80=99t great in a painting, can you start shorting =
shares of it to drive down the trading price, and then buy the whole painti=
ng cheap at a premium to the manipulated market price? Even better: Divide =
a $55 million painting into 1 million shares. Sell 500,000 of them in an IP=
O and keep the rest. They start trading on the exchange, but somewhat sleep=
ily. You start dumping some more shares, sloppily: Say you sell 100,000 sha=
res over 20 days, driving the average price down to $40. (You have sold 600=
,000 shares for total proceeds of about 30.7 million: $55 times 500,000 min=
us a 3% fee is $26.7 million in the IPO, plus $4 million from selling 100,0=
00 shares at an average price of $40.) Then you put in a bid to buy the who=
le painting at $48 (a 20% premium to the trading price), no one outbids you=
 in this sleepy odd market that you have manipulated, and you pay $28.8 mil=
lion for the 600,000 shares you don=E2=80=99t own, a round-trip profit of a=
bout $1.9 million, minus some transaction costs. And you get the painting b=
ack.

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=

5px;">K5</h2>
</td>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For a while crypto was probably the highest bi=
dder for celebrity and influence. A ton of money flowed into crypto over th=
e last few years. Margins were high; it=E2=80=99s not like crypto firms had=
 to build a lot of factories and hire a lot of workers to make new tokens. =
Crypto went up based on social adoption, on widespread respectability, on t=
he prospect=C2=A0of increasing legality. If you were a crypto firm in 2022,=
 funneling gobs of money to politicians and former regulators and celebriti=
es was a really good investment in making your firm popular and respectable=
, and it didn=E2=80=99t really compete that much with=C2=A0<em>other=C2=A0<=
/em>investments. If you are Apple Inc., it is good to have regulators on yo=
ur side and celebrities endorsing your products, but also you make phones a=
nd if you spend a lot of money making the phones better then customers will=
 want more of them. For large parts of the crypto ecosystem that, uh, was n=
ot really an option. Influencer marketing and political lobbying were valua=
ble and viable; making useful products that appealed to consumers in an imm=
ediate and intuitive way was not.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so here=C2=A0is <a href=3D"https://link.ma=
il.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0aW1lcy5j=
b20vMjAyMy8wNi8yMy90ZWNobm9sb2d5L3NhbS1iYW5rbWFuLWZyaWVkLWNlbGVicml0eS1mcml=
lbmRzLmh0bWw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBb1ab10e0" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" item=
scope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorat=
ion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00000=
0; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> a New York Times s=
tory</a> about some guys who were in the celebrity-and-influence business a=
nd were at the top of their game:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A former aide to President Clinton, [Michael] =
Kives started working for Creative Artists Agency in 2003, representing act=
ors like Bruce Willis and Arnold Schwarzenegger. At Mr. Kives=E2=80=99s 201=
9 wedding in Palm Springs, Calif., [Katy] Perry sang =E2=80=9CHava Nagila,=
=E2=80=9D a Jewish folk song, and two writers on =E2=80=9CThe Office=E2=80=
=9D delivered a roast.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mr. Kives started K5 in 2018. He was later joi=
ned by [Bryan] Baum, an entrepreneur who had co-founded a software company =
that was acquired by Lyft. K5 has invested in start-ups including SpaceX an=
d Boring Company, which are run by Elon Musk, amassing a portfolio of 148 c=
ompanies.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As they built K5, Mr. Kives and Mr. Baum toute=
d their connections, including with the billionaire financier Warren E. Buf=
fett.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: =
transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a></p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Their firm, K5 Global, is an investment firm, =
and I suppose that running an investment firm is one way to monetize having=
 a lot of connections to the rich and famous: Your connections can help wit=
h raising money and also with getting into hot deals; =E2=80=9CI am good fr=
iends with Elon Musk=E2=80=9D is one way to get differentiated (<a href=3D"=
https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d=
3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTAtMDMvZXZlcnlvbmUtd2Fu=
dGVkLXRvLWJ1eS10d2l0dGVyLXdpdGgtZWxvbj9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNjI2MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ=
1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA2MjYmdX=
RtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBc0cb3a97" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">
not always good!</a>) investment performance in recent years.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But a better way to monetize those connections=
 was apparently to <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click=
/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0aW1lcy5jb20vMjAyMy8wNi8yMy90ZWNobm9sb2=
d5L3NhbS1iYW5rbWFuLWZyaWVkLWNlbGVicml0eS1mcmllbmRzLmh0bWw/607f07ceef4b8524a=
319a23cCb1ab10e0" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"=
_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-b=
ottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid;=
 background-color: #ccc;"> sell them to Sam Bankman-Fried</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mr. Kives, a Hollywood agent turned investor, =
played an unusual role in Mr. Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s business empire: supe=
r connector. He and his business partner, Bryan Baum, helped the young foun=
der cultivate relationships with Mr. Bloom, Ms. Perry and former President =
Bill Clinton, and offered introductions to a who=E2=80=99s who of celebriti=
es and business leaders, from Leonardo DiCaprio to the governor of Saudi Ar=
abia=E2=80=99s Public Investment Fund.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The relationship was mutually beneficial. Mr. =
Bankman-Fried invested $700 million in Mr. Kives=E2=80=99s venture-capital =
firm, court records show, an extraordinary level of support for a fund with=
 a short track record of start-up investments. Mr. Kives, the founder and f=
ace of the firm, and Mr. Baum each received $125 million as part of the dea=
l.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mr. Kives, 42, and Mr. Baum, 34, were part of =
an under-the-radar network of middlemen who introduced celebrities and othe=
r power brokers to the once high-flying crypto industry. Before cryptocurre=
ncies crashed last year, a drumbeat of endorsements from Hollywood stars, p=
rofessional athletes, business titans and politicians created an aura of cr=
edibility around the volatile and largely unregulated market. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On Thursday, FTX=E2=80=99s bankruptcy lawyers =
sued Mr. Kives=E2=80=99s firm, K5 Global, to recover the money that Mr. Ban=
kman-Fried had invested. The $700 million was among the largest sums the FT=
X founder contributed to any group; the lawsuit said he had made the invest=
ment =E2=80=9Cto burnish his own political and social influence.=E2=80=9D</=
p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One hundred twenty-five million dollars! Each!=
 <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31906837.263401/a=
HR0cHM6Ly9yZXN0cnVjdHVyaW5nLnJhLmtyb2xsLmNvbS9GVFgvSG9tZS1Eb3dubG9hZFBERj9p=
ZDE9TVRVek16YzNNQT09JmlkMj0tMQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB75c436ad" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
 The lawsuit</a> makes it clear that FTX paid them that money for some of t=
heir shares in their businesses, not as=C2=A0personal commissions, but it c=
omes to about the same place. =E2=80=9CNo due diligence or financial analys=
is was conducted to support this inflated valuation=E2=80=9D for the K5 bus=
inesses, says the complaint,=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cand Kives and Baum knew that thi=
s valuation was not well founded and was materially inflated.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The complaint is generally a fun read:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Kives and Bankman-Fried first connected via em=
ail on November 16, 2021, when Kives helped introduce Bankman-Fried to worl=
d-renowned Australian musician Sia. Shortly thereafter, Kives began invitin=
g Bankman-Fried to dinner parties with Wall Street titans, Academy Award wi=
nning actors, and NBA stars. On February 11, 2022, the Friday before the Su=
per Bowl, Bankman-Fried attended a dinner party at Kives=E2=80=99s house al=
ongside former high-ranking politicians, a centibillionaire CEO, and variou=
s other celebrities. The next day, February 12, 2022, Kives connected Bankm=
an-Fried via email to a tech company CEO and his wife, a former U.S. Ambass=
ador to the Bahamas.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On February 13, 2022, Bankman-Fried attended t=
he Super Bowl as Kives=E2=80=99s guest, along with A-list celebrities. Bank=
man-Fried was captivated by Kives=E2=80=99s star-studded circle of friends,=
 and two days later raved in an internal document that K5 Global was =E2=80=
=9Csomething of a one-stop shop for relationships that we should utilize.=
=E2=80=9D Bankman-Fried noted that Kives and Baum wanted several things in =
exchange, including =E2=80=9C[m]aybe us to invest in them,=E2=80=9D and, as=
 described below, such investments quickly followed.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Alameda Research Ltd., Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s=
 trading firm, invested about $300 million in K5 entities and about $400 mi=
llion in some K5 funds, which made venture investments with the money:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As of September 18, 2022, K5X Fund=
 I LP had invested $280.5 million. The vast majority=E2=80=94$252 million=
=E2=80=94was invested in just eight companies, two of which were SpaceX ($1=
89.7 million invested) and The Boring Company ($23 million invested), both =
founded by Elon Musk, with whom Kives purported to be close friends. These =
two investments represented approximately 76% of K5X Fund I LP=E2=80=99s po=
rtfolio.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bankman-Fried noted in an internal document th=
at the FTX executive had questioned whether Baum was acting in FTX=E2=80=99=
s best interest and had suggested that this =E2=80=9Creek[ed]=E2=80=9D of B=
aum =E2=80=9Ctrying to nickel and dime FTX, or scam it a little bit . . . .=
=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Despite these concerns, Bankman-Fried defended=
 Baum, describing what he believed was the root of the issue: =E2=80=9C<em>=
Is Bryan an FTX employee, or a random 3rd party?</em> The answer, really, i=
s<em> neither</em>. The answer is that it=E2=80=99s sorta complicated and l=
iminal and unclear. Bryan lives in the uncanny valley=E2=80=9D (emphasis in=
 original).</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Great.=C2=A0In bankruptcy, a company can in so=
me circumstances=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/cl=
ick/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubGF3LmNvcm5lbGwuZWR1L3VzY29kZS90ZXh0LzE=
xLzU0OA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3dd53ec3" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">get back money that it p=
aid</a> to other companies, if it =E2=80=9Creceived less than a reasonably =
equivalent value in exchange for such transfer or obligation=E2=80=9D and w=
as insolvent at the time. And so the FTX bankruptcy estate is suing Kives, =
Baum and K5 for the money back, because it =E2=80=9Cdid not receive reasona=
bly equivalent value in exchange for=E2=80=9D all the money it sent them. W=
hich seems right! One hundred twenty-five million dollars! Each! For some p=
arty invites!</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On the other hand:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Ex ante, it is possible that trying t=
o become friends with like Bill Clinton and Elon Musk and the head of the P=
IF and Sia was the best possible use of FTX=E2=80=99s $700 million. In the =
last few years, big crypto firms were in a race to achieve respectability a=
nd stability before they could be brought down by legal problems and/or by =
stealing all the money; FTX <em>lost</em> the race, but I=E2=80=99m not sur=
e it was unreasonable for it to pour all of its resources into running it. =
What else was it gonna do?</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Most of=
 what FTX/Alameda did, as they slid into bankruptcy, took the form of spend=
ing=C2=A0real customer money on piles of magic beans. At the end, FTX was l=
eft with a <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3190683=
7.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMT=
EtMTQvZnR4LXMtYmFsYW5jZS1zaGVldC13YXMtYmFkP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA2MjYyM19NT05FWVNUV=

rop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color:=
 #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; borde=
r-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc=
;">  balance sheet</a> consisting mostly of Serum and MAPS and FTT and othe=
r quasi-worthless tokens that Bankman-Fried made up. I suppose the stakes i=
n K5 are similarly magic-bean-ish, but=C2=A0at least=C2=A0<em>some=C2=A0</e=
m>of the K5 stuff was real! FTX put money into K5's funds and=C2=A0apparent=
ly got, like, $189.7 million worth of SpaceX, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.=
bloombergbusiness.com/click/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmN=

or FTT.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The complaint says that FT=

ckground-color: #ccc;">  say</a> that all cryptocurrency exchanges are doin=
g crimes, and if you=E2=80=99re lucky your exchange is doing only process c=
rimes. But what if it is? Consider this simple hypothetical fact pattern:</=
p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">A crypto exchange has $1 billion of c=
ustomer money.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It has stolen none =
of this money! Good job.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The excha=
nge also has $50 million of its own money, which it very carefully keeps se=
parate from the customer money, with no stealing allowed.</li>=09<li style=
=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The US Securities and Exchange Commission fines it=
 $100 million for operating an illegal securities exchange.</li>=09<li styl=
e=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Now the exchange owes customers $1 billion and th=
e SEC $100 million, for a total of $1.1 billion.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin=
-bottom: 5px;">It has $1 billion of customer money and $50 million of its o=
wn money, for a total of $1.05 billion, which is less.</li>=09<li style=3D"=
margin-bottom: 5px;">Its liabilities exceed its assets, so it files for ban=
kruptcy.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Do the customers get paid=
 in full, or the SEC, or neither?</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This problem comes up because =E2=80=9Ccustome=
r money" is a fuzzier concept in crypto than it is in traditional banking o=
r securities brokerage. When you send dollars to a crypto exchange and use =
them to buy Bitcoins, and you look at your balance on the exchange's websit=
e and it says =E2=80=9C1.57 Bitcoins=E2=80=9D or whatever, you tend to thin=
k that those Bitcoins belong to you. Frankly the exchange would probably=C2=
=A0<em>prefer=C2=A0</em>that those Bitcoins belong to you, for marketing an=
d legal reasons.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the situation is not particularly clear, a=
nd it does seem more likely that the exchange=C2=A0<em>owns=C2=A0</em>the B=
itcoins, and just=C2=A0<em>owes=C2=A0</em>you an unsecured debt: If the exc=
hange doesn=E2=80=99t have enough money to pay back all its creditors, you =
may not get back all your Bitcoins. =E2=80=9CBecause custodially held crypt=
o assets may be considered to be the property of a bankruptcy estate, in th=
e event of a bankruptcy, the crypto assets we hold in custody on behalf of =
our customers could be subject to bankruptcy proceedings and such customers=
 could be treated as our general unsecured creditors,=E2=80=9D <a href=3D"h=
ttps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3=
cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL2RhdGEvMTY3OTc4OC8wMDAxNjc5Nzg4M=

OTc3XzI2NQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB344fa4ac" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemsc=
ope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoratio=
n: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000;=
 border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> says Coinbase Global=
 Inc.</a>, controversially but correctly. And in fact lots of crypto busine=
sses have gone bankrupt in the last year or so, and it <a href=3D"https://l=
ink.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb2=
1iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTItMDYveW91LWNhbi10LXRlbGwtYW5hb=

pe=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration=
: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; =
border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">
does seem like</a> the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/c=
lick/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbG=
VzLzIwMjMtMDEtMDUvY29pbmRlYWwtcy1iZW50bGV5cy13ZXJlbi10LXJlYWw_Y21waWQ9QkJEM=
DYyNjIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXIm=
dXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNjI2JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a2=
3cB49398e11" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blan=
k" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom=
-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; back=
ground-color: #ccc;">
legal result</a> is mostly that the coins belong to the crypto businesses a=
nd the customers are unsecured creditors. Which might mean that they would =
have to share with the SEC.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Of course the other question here is, would th=
e SEC actually do this? Would the SEC fine a crypto exchange=C2=A0<em>more =
than all of its own money</em> for illegally trading securities, and go aft=
er customers for the rest of the money? On the one hand, it seems a little =
crazy: When it goes after crypto exchanges for operating illegal securities=
 exchanges, the SEC=E2=80=99s goal is presumably to=C2=A0<em>protect custom=
ers </em>(by imposing=C2=A0on crypto exchanges the disclosure, conflicts, s=
upervision, etc. rules applicable to securities exchanges), and it would be=
 strange to take customer money as a penalty for customer-protection failin=
gs. On the other hand, I mean, surely the SEC=E2=80=99s=C2=A0<em>broader=C2=
=A0</em>goal is to=C2=A0<em>clamp down on crypto</em>, and sending a messag=
e to crypto customers like =E2=80=9Cyour money is not safe at any crypto ex=
change, because the SEC might take it=E2=80=9D has a certain deterrent effe=
ct.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway this is all a=C2=A0<em>bit=C2=A0</em>fa=
nciful, but also a bit not. In April <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlu=

putting it into bankruptcy</a>. But all of the customer=C2=A0money is there=
 and accounted for, and Bittrex asked the bankruptcy court to let it proces=
s customer withdrawals normally.=C2=A0The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=
mbergbusiness.com/click/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly9wZXRpdGlvbi5zdWJzdGFjay5=

ext-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-co=
lor: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Petition=
 newsletter</a> reports:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The debtors filed a motion seeking=
 entry of an order authorizing them to honor withdrawals of crypto by custo=
mers. Interestingly, the debtors stated: =E2=80=9Cthere may be a dispute be=
tween the Debtors and their customers as to who owns the cryptocurrencies d=
eposited by customers and stored in the Debtors=E2=80=99 wallets. The Debto=
rs believe that they own the cryptocurrency assets. But, the proposed treat=
ment, where customers will be permitted to withdraw cryptocurrencies equiva=
lent to 100% of the cryptocurrencies they deposited as a distribution (subj=
ect to complying with information requests to ensure that all governmental =
rules and regulations are satisfied) avoids the expense and delay of litiga=
tion concerning ownership of those assets, while still providing customers,=
 the vast majority of which are individuals, the ability to receive a 100% =
distribution by withdrawing the cryptocurrencies.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is: The customers technically have an uns=
ecured claim on their crypto assets, which are technically owned by Bittrex=
. But there=E2=80=99s plenty of money and not much debt so it=E2=80=99s fin=
e, Bittrex will just give them their=C2=A0crypto back anyway.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Except that the SEC is still suing Bittrex, an=
d presumably expects to extract a large fine. And so the question arises: C=
an Bittrex give its customers back all their money, or does it have to hang=
 on to some of that money to=C2=A0<em>potentially use to pay SEC fines? </e=
m>While the bankruptcy judge did sign the order allowing Bittrex to give it=
s customers their money back, the order explicitly said that it would not=
=C2=A0</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">prohibit the Debtors, any liquidat=
ing trustee, plan administrator or any creditor, including the United State=
s, from recovering from any customer the value of any cryptocurrency assets=
 or fiat currency such customer receives that exceeds the distributions pro=
vided by the plan, if such general unsecured creditor or subordinated credi=
tor is not paid in full, and, in such event, the entry of this Order shall =
not constitute collateral estoppel or res judicata of any customers' entitl=
ement to cryptocurrency assets or priority under any federal or state law.<=
/p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is: If the SEC is not paid in full on wha=
tever fines it ultimately extracts, it can go after Bittrex=E2=80=99s custo=
mers for the amounts they withdrew. As Petition says =E2=80=9CSoooooo=E2=80=
=A6customers can withdraw their crypto but that crypto is subject to a pote=
ntial clawback in favor of the regulators? FUN!!=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Again this i=
s all a bit fanciful, and I cannot really imagine the SEC suing Bittrex=E2=
=80=99s customers for withdrawing their money. But it=E2=80=99s keeping the=
 option open.</p>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I think sometimes about the similarities betwe=
en these three businesses:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Journalism</li>=09<li style=3D"margin=
-bottom: 5px;">Spying</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Fundamental =
investment analysis</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The basic job of many journalists and spies, a=
nd of a certain sort of analyst, is to develop personal relationships and u=
se them to find out useful information that no one else knows, and then giv=
e that information to your customers. If you are a journalist your customer=
s are your readers =E2=80=94=C2=A0sometimes =E2=80=9Cthe public,=E2=80=9D b=
ut sometimes a rather narrower class of paying subscribers. If you are a sp=
y your customer is your government. If you are an investment analyst, your =
customer might be your hedge fund (your boss and your clients), or you migh=
t be producing research for outside customers (basically,=C2=A0<em>several=
=C2=A0</em>hedge funds).</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The boundaries can be porous. There are some=
=C2=A0<em>very=C2=A0</em>niche financial publications, for instance. Consid=
er this fact pattern:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You work for a publication.</li>=09<li=
 style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You cultivate deep relationships with the em=
ployees of a publicly traded company.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5p=
x;">They tell you that the company is cooking its books, and they smuggle o=
ut corporate documents proving it.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;"=
>You write up your findings in a big detailed convincing report, put the he=
adline =E2=80=9CCompany X Is a Fraud=E2=80=9D on it,=C2=A0and publish it fo=
r your readers.</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you work at Bloomberg News or the New York =
Times, and your readers are millions of subscribers and website visitors, t=
his is pretty clearly =E2=80=9Cjournalism,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0and good. If you w=
ork at a firm called Specialized Stock Research and your readers are two he=
dge funds who each pay $1 million a year for your reports, this is pretty c=
learly =E2=80=9Cinvestment research,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0and regulators might hav=
e serious questions about whether it is=C2=A0<em>insider trading.=C2=A0</em=
>Insiders at a public company leaked information to you because of your lon=
g-standing relationship, and then you passed that information on to people =
who traded on it: That sounds a lot like a crime.<a class=3D"footnote-index=
" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display=
: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>=C2=A0I wonder sometimes what the boundary is: Is there some minimum nu=
mber of subscribers, or some maximum subscription price, that makes a repor=
t =E2=80=9Cjournalism=E2=80=9D rather than =E2=80=9Cinsider trading=E2=80=
=9D?<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #00000=
0; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-botto=
m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: t=
ransparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Similarly if your job is to work in China and =
find out stuff about Chinese companies and policymakers and report back wha=
t you learned to an American audience, you might be a spy (if the audience =
is the American government) or a journalist (American newspaper readers) or=
 a due diligence consultant (American investors). But in each case your act=
ivities will be kind of similar, and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQvMjE2=
MzQ1OTEtZWQ1My00ZDgzLTk2NGQtODAyYmVhYTdmYzY0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5acf1=
dea" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">subject to misinterpretation</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Expert network consulting company Gerson Lehrm=
an Group has become the latest due diligence firm to cut jobs in China as B=
eijing intensifies scrutiny of the sector on national security grounds.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">US-based GLG, which maintains a network of spe=
cialists that global investors can tap for due diligence on transactions, b=
egan trimming China staff last month, said several people familiar with the=
 matter.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The reductions come as Beijing cracks down on =
foreign consultancies this year, alarming international investors at a time=
 of growing tensions between the US and China. The campaign has made operat=
ing in China more difficult for foreign companies, which depend on the cons=
ultants to help navigate the world=E2=80=99s second-largest economy. =E2=80=
=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Investors and foreign multinationals said the =
crackdown made performing due diligence for investments and procurement con=
tracts with Chinese partners and suppliers difficult.</p>
</blockquote>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If a painting is worth $55 million, how much i=
s a tradeable=C2=A0one-millionth fractional share of the painting worth? Th=
ree possible answers:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">$55, because $55 million divided by 1=
 million is $55.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">More than $55, be=
cause there are more people in the world with $55 than with $55 million, an=
d they will all want shares, and a deep liquid publicly traded pool of buye=
rs will make the shares worth more than the painting itself.</li>=09<li sty=
le=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Less than $55, because if you buy the painting f=
or $55 million you can hang it over your couch, while if you buy a share fo=
r $55 you can=E2=80=99t hang anything anywhere.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99t know which is right. Intuitive=
ly, it seems to me that owning an entire painting offers certain aesthetic =
and prestige benefits, while owning any fraction of a painting does not off=
er any fraction of those benefits: Hanging nothing over your couch is not o=
ne one-millionth as valuable, interior-decor-wise, as hanging a fancy paint=
ing over your couch. But I have been wrong before, and the rise of crypto a=
nd <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31906837.263401=
/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDktMDkvdH=
dlbnR5LXBlcmNlbnQtb2YtYS1waWN0dXJlLW9mLWEtZG9nP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA2MjYyM19NT05FW=
VNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIz=
MDYyNiZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBc5f7b3b8" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;">
non-fungible tokens</a> does apparently teach the lesson that people will p=
ay for, and derive some sort of prestige and aesthetic benefit from, an ent=
ry in a ledger saying that they own a tiny fraction of some object that the=
y cannot touch or look at.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also there is a sort of soft no-arbitrage cond=
ition, which is that if the shares all trade at, say, $30, then somebody co=
uld buy them all for $30 million, own the entire painting, and hang it over=
 her couch, at a significant discount to its real value of $55 million.<a c=
lass=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-=
decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color:=
 #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transpare=
nt !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9pc=
G8tZnJhbmNpcy1iYWNvbi1wb3J0cmFpdC1nZW9yZ2UtZHllci1hcnQtbWFya2V0LTRhNjYwMDBh=
/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB34926f42" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"ite=
mscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !i=
mportant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bo=
ttom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> here=E2=80=99s this</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A company named Artex is launching a roughly $=
55 million initial public offering of Bacon=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9CThree Studie=
s for a Portrait of George Dyer,=E2=80=9D painted shortly after the couple =
met in 1963. Shares in the portrait will be sold for around $100 a piece an=
d will list on a specially created art stock exchange based in Liechtenstei=
n, giving regular investors the ability to buy and sell shares in a famous =
artwork on a stock exchange for the first time.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Trading is expected to begin in July. The port=
rait will then go on public display in a museum, rather than be squirreled =
away in a freeport =E2=80=94 high-security, low-tax warehouses where wealth=
y collectors often store valuable works of art.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Part of the pitch here is about reducing trans=
action costs:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Artex has big plans to float $1 bi=
llion worth of paintings over the next few months, each individual work wit=
h its own IPO. The company thinks supply will come from private collectors =
that are looking for a cheaper alternative to selling through auction house=
s like Sotheby=E2=80=99s and Christie=E2=80=99s, which can charge up to 20%=
 in commission. Artex takes a lower cut of 3% of the IPO proceeds plus a sm=
all fee every time shares in the Bacon portrait change hands.</p></blockquo=
te>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I assume that if you are Sotheby=E2=80=99s or =
Christie=E2=80=99s, your pitch to collectors is =E2=80=9Cwe will sell your =
painting to rich art collectors with whom we have cultivated relationships =
and who want to hang it in their houses, which will result in a price that =
is at least 25% higher than you=E2=80=99d get by selling=C2=A0fractional sh=
ares in your painting to online randos who=C2=A0<em>can=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</em=
>hang it in their houses.=E2=80=9D I don=E2=80=99t know if that is correct,=
 though, and it=E2=80=99s hard to measure without much data.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Bacon portrait also should be =
a bit easier to cash in and out of as it will be publicly traded, and Artex=
 will subsidize up to 3% of market value daily to provide liquidity.</p></b=
lockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><em>In general</em>, =E2=80=9Cwe will open up =
an exchange for X targeting retail investors, and we will also be a market =
maker on that exchange, buying X when retail investors want to sell it and =
selling it when they want to buy it=E2=80=9D seems like a risky but usually=
 lucrative business. (It=E2=80=99s a rough description of the modern <a hre=
f=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31906837.263401/aHR0cHM6=
Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDItMDUvcm9iaW5ob29=
kLWdhbWVzdG9wLXNhZ2EtcHJlc3N1cmVzLXBheW1lbnQtZm9yLW9yZGVyLWZsb3c_Y21waWQ9Qk=
JEMDYyNjIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0Z=
XImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNjI2JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a31=
9a23cBdee1849c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_b=
lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;">
US retail stock brokerage business</a>; it has also made a lot of fortunes =
in crypto.) If you are Sotheby=E2=80=99s, the big problem with $55 million =
paintings is that they trade infrequently (though at huge spreads). If you =
are Artex and you can succeed in making this Bacon painting trade dozens of=
 times a day (though in small fractional size and at tighter spreads), and =
you get to be in the middle of each trade, that could be a good deal.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The first publicly listed artwork =
also raises the tantalizing possibility of a takeover of the Bacon portrait=
. If a billionaire collector or museum wants to swoop in with a bid, they m=
ust offer a 20% premium to the average closing price of the shares over the=
 previous 20 trading days, according to rules set by Artex. A rival bidder =
can then step in and offer even more, though unlike with a typical corporat=
e takeover, shareholders don=E2=80=99t get to vote on whether to accept the=
 offer.=C2=A0</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is a weird mechanism? Does it imply that =
the painting=C2=A0<em>ought=C2=A0</em>to trade at a discount to its =E2=80=
=9Creal=E2=80=9D value in private hands (because any private buyer would ha=
ve to pay a 20% premium)? If you are a rich collector, would you rather buy=
 a painting from Artex at a 20% premium to its trading price, or at a Chris=
tie=E2=80=99s auction with a 20% commission?<a class=3D"footnote-index" hre=
f=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important=
; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-sty=
le: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inl=
ine-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a></p>

-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  true for corporate stocks =
too</a>, by the way: If a company=E2=80=99s stock trades for much less than=

<div id=3D"footnote-5" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[5] Also can you manipulate this automatic-fix=
ed-premium, no-vote mechanism? If liquidity isn=E2=80=99t great in a painti=
ng, can you start shorting shares of it to drive down the trading price, an=
d then buy the whole painting cheap at a premium to the manipulated market =
price? Even better: Divide a $55 million painting into 1 million shares. Se=
ll 500,000 of them in an IPO and keep the rest. They start trading on the e=
xchange, but somewhat sleepily. You start dumping some more shares, sloppil=
y: Say you sell 100,000 shares over 20 days, driving the average price down=
 to $40. (You have sold 600,000 shares for total proceeds of about 30.7 mil=
lion: $55 times 500,000 minus a 3% fee is $26.7 million in the IPO, plus $4=
 million from selling 100,000 shares at an average price of $40.) Then you =
put in a bid to buy the whole painting at $48 (a 20% premium to the trading=
 price), no one outbids you in this sleepy odd market that you have manipul=
ated, and you pay $28.8 million for the 600,000 shares you don=E2=80=99t ow=
n, a round-trip profit of about $1.9 million, minus some transaction costs.=
 And you get the painting back.</p>
</div>
